dosageInstruction.text: 4 mg IV push once for nausea while NPO, per ED triage protocol

transcript:
NURSE: Priya Raman? And your date of birth — January thirtieth, ninety-nine?
PT: Yeah. That's me.
NURSE: Have a seat. What's the main problem today?
PT: My stomach. It's this really sharp pain, low on my right side. It's been going on like twelve hours and it's just getting worse.
NURSE: When did it start?
PT: This morning. It started around my belly button, then this afternoon it moved down to the right and stayed there.
NURSE: Okay. Zero to ten, right now?
PT: Seven. It was like a four this morning. Walking makes it worse — every bump in the car on the way here was awful. My roommate drove me.
NURSE: Mm-hm. Is it constant, or does it come and go?
PT: Constant. Lying really still helps a little. And I've been nauseous since this afternoon — zero appetite.
NURSE: Any vomiting?
PT: No. I felt hot and kind of shivery tonight, but I never threw up.
NURSE: Any burning when you pee, or any vaginal bleeding?
PT: No. Neither.
NURSE: When did you last eat or drink anything?
PT: Noon. Half a sandwich. So about eight hours ago.
NURSE: First day of your last period?
PT: About three weeks ago. I'm bad at tracking it. And we're not always careful, if that matters.
NURSE: Okay. Any allergies to medications?
PT: Amoxicillin. Rash all over when I was a kid.
NURSE: Any medications you take regularly — prescriptions, birth control, anything over the counter?
PT: No, nothing. Um — I don't really have a pharmacy right now. My MassHealth got cut off while I was in county, and the re-enrollment hasn't gone through yet. No doctor either. I've never had surgery or anything, though.
NURSE: Okay. Cuff's going to squeeze. Temp 37.9 — that's a low-grade fever. Pressure 118 over 76, heart rate 92, breathing 16, oxygen 99 percent on room air. And step on the scale for me — 61 kilos.
PT: Okay.
NURSE: Quick screening questions, same ones for everyone. Do you feel safe at home? Any thoughts of hurting yourself in the past few weeks?
PT: I'm safe, yeah. And no, nothing like that.
NURSE: We offer an HIV test to every patient — it runs with the blood work you'll likely get anyway. Want it added today?
PT: Um — sure. Yeah.
NURSE: Done. Last thing — I need a urine sample. The cup's in that bathroom; we run a urinalysis and a pregnancy test on it for belly pain. Then head back out to the waiting room and we'll call you when a bed opens. Nothing to eat or drink until a doctor sees you, okay?
PT: Okay. Thank you.
note:
**Subjective:** 27-year-old woman with sharp right lower quadrant pain since this morning, about twelve hours before arrival. The pain began around the umbilicus, moved to the right lower quadrant this afternoon, and has stayed there — constant, 7/10 now from about 4/10 at onset, worse with walking and car bumps, slightly better lying still. She reports nausea since this afternoon with no appetite and denies vomiting, burning with urination, and vaginal bleeding. She felt hot and shivery at home this evening. Last oral intake was half a sandwich at noon, about eight hours ago. Last menstrual period was about three weeks ago and she states her tracking is unreliable. Allergy: amoxicillin (childhood rash). She takes no medications and has had no surgeries. Safety and suicide screens were negative, and she accepted the universal opt-out HIV test.

**Objective:** T 37.9 C, HR 92/min, BP 118/76 mmHg, RR 16/min, SpO2 99% on room air, weight 61 kg, pain 7/10. Alert, uncomfortable, ambulatory.

**Assessment and Plan:** Migratory periumbilical-to-RLQ pain with low-grade fever, nausea, and anorexia — possible acute appendicitis; pregnancy not excluded. ESI level 3. Per abdominal-pain protocol a urine specimen was collected for urinalysis and point-of-care hCG, and she was instructed to remain NPO. Assigned to the Main ED queue and returned to the waiting room pending a bed; recheck vitals and pain q2h and return to triage immediately for rigidity, syncope, uncontrolled pain, or a positive hCG.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• You came in with sharp pain low on the right side of your belly that started this morning, along with nausea and a low-grade fever (37.9 C).
• This pattern can be caused by appendicitis, so the emergency team is checking it carefully tonight.
• A urine test, including a pregnancy check, was collected at triage.
• You accepted the routine HIV test we offer to everyone — it runs with your blood work, no extra needle.
• Your MassHealth re-enrollment is still pending — you will be treated fully tonight regardless of coverage, and a social worker can help move the paperwork along.

Next steps
• Please do not eat or drink anything until the doctor says it is okay.
• Wait in the waiting room — we will call you as soon as a bed opens.
• Blood tests and likely a CT scan of your belly will follow once the doctor sees you.
• Tell the front desk right away if the pain suddenly gets much worse, you feel faint, or you start vomiting.

fields.value: None — no prescriptions, contraceptive pills, or OTC medications

fields.value: ~3 weeks ago (approx. 2026-06-27); patient states tracking is unreliable

fields.value: Negative — denies thoughts of self-harm in the past few weeks

fields.label: Insurance coverage
fields.value: No active coverage — MassHealth lapsed during county incarceration; re-enrollment submitted and still pending (volunteered during medication check)

fields.value: No PCP and no pharmacy at present — volunteered in one brief aside; nurse did not pursue

fields.label: Support system / transportation
fields.value: Roommate drove her in — transportation and some home support inferred from brief mention

esi.label: ESI 3 — Urgent
esi.rationale: Decision point A is negative — no immediate lifesaving intervention required. Decision point B is negative — no GI bleeding, syncope, or new confusion reported; pain 7/10 without severe distress; hemodynamically stable, though ectopic pregnancy is not excluded pending the POC urine hCG collected at triage. At decision point C she is expected to use two or more resources (labs, urinalysis with hCG, likely CT abdomen/pelvis, IV therapy), placing her at ESI level 3. Decision point D does not force an uptriage: T 37.9 C, HR 92, RR 16, SpO2 99% RA are outside danger-zone criteria.

decision_points.a_lifesaving: Negative — airway, breathing, and circulation intact; no immediate lifesaving intervention required
decision_points.b_high_risk: Negative — no GI bleeding, syncope, or severe distress reported; hemodynamically stable; ectopic not yet excluded, POC hCG collected and pending
decision_points.c_resources: 2+ resources anticipated — labs (CBC/BMP/lipase), urinalysis with POC hCG, CT abdomen/pelvis, IV fluids/antiemetic
decision_points.d_vital_signs: No danger-zone vitals — T 37.9 C, HR 92, RR 16, SpO2 99% RA; no uptriage from 3 to 2

routing.bed: Pending — waiting room until a Main ED bed opens
routing.monitoring: Waiting room with comfort rounds; recheck vitals and pain q2h; return to triage immediately for rigidity, syncope, uncontrolled pain, or a positive POC hCG

resources.rationale: Exclude UTI; pregnancy not ruled out (LMP ~3 weeks, inconsistent contraception volunteered) — hCG required before contrast CT and to assess ectopic risk

resources.name: CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast
resources.rationale: Migratory periumbilical-to-RLQ pain with low-grade fever and anorexia — evaluate for acute appendicitis; release pending negative hCG

resources.name: IV access + antiemetic
resources.rationale: Active nausea while NPO; line also supports fluids and CT contrast

resources.name: NPO pending surgical evaluation
resources.rationale: Possible appendicitis — keep NPO in case operative management is needed tonight

resources.name: Social work consult
resources.rationale: MassHealth lapsed during recent county incarceration with re-enrollment pending; expedite coverage re-enrollment and arrange PCP/clinic linkage so surgical follow-up and discharge medications are not derailed by the coverage gap

resources.name: HIV screening (opt-out offer accepted)
resources.rationale: Universal opt-out ED HIV screening offered to every patient; accepted — run off tonight's blood draw, result returns with labs

routing.rationale: Stable ESI 3 patient with a classic migratory-RLQ appendicitis story: she needs a Main ED bed where labs, imaging, and IV therapy can run in parallel — too resource-heavy for fast track, no telemetry indication — and waits in the waiting room until one opens. Social work is part of the clinical plan, not an add-on: her coverage gap is the biggest threat to follow-through after tonight, and untreated appendicitis is exactly the emergency that coverage gaps turn catastrophic.

triage.physician_note:
**S:**
- 27-year-old woman presenting with ~12 hours of right lower quadrant abdominal pain.
- Onset this morning, ~12 hours before arrival; pain began around the umbilicus, moved to the right lower quadrant this afternoon, and has stayed there (volunteered, unprompted).
- Sharp quality; constant, does not come and go. Progressive per patient — about 4/10 this morning, 7/10 now. Worse with walking and with every car bump en route; lying completely still helps a little.
- Nausea since this afternoon with "zero appetite"; felt hot and shivery at home tonight.
- Pertinent ROS positives (spoken): sharp RLQ pain, nausea, anorexia, subjective fever/chills.
- Pertinent ROS negatives (spoken): denies vomiting; denies burning with urination; denies vaginal bleeding.
- Reported history: LMP about three weeks ago — states she is "bad at tracking it" and volunteered "we're not always careful." Allergy to amoxicillin (rash all over as a kid). No home medications — no prescriptions, birth control, or OTC. Never had surgery. Last oral intake half a sandwich at noon, ~8 hours before arrival.
- Reported social: MassHealth cut off while in county, re-enrollment not yet through; no doctor and no pharmacy at present. Roommate drove her in. States she feels safe at home; denies thoughts of self-harm in the past few weeks. Accepted the universal opt-out HIV test.

**O:**
- 20:41 vitals: T 37.9 C, HR 92, BP 118/76, RR 16, SpO2 99% RA, pain 7/10; weight 61 kg (20:43).
- 20:58 recheck: T 37.8 C, HR 88, BP 116/74, RR 16, SpO2 99% RA, pain 7/10.
- Alert, answering in full sentences without respiratory distress; ambulatory — walked to the scale at triage.
- Urine specimen collected at triage; urinalysis and point-of-care hCG still pending at end of triage.
- No analgesic or antiemetic administered during triage; pain unchanged at 7/10 on recheck.

analysis: Patient is terse and guarded — clipped replies ("Yeah. That's me.", "No. Neither.", "Noon. Half a sandwich."), detail surfacing mostly under direct questioning, and a hedged aside ("...if that matters") when the contraception topic comes up.

The coverage-lapse disclosure comes haltingly ("Um — I don't really have a pharmacy right now") before she mentions county incarceration and the lapsed MassHealth; the nurse's flat "Okay." is neutral and nonjudgmental — a positive marker — but she pivots straight to the blood-pressure cuff without acknowledging it.

The nurse's pacing is procedural end to end — question, minimal acknowledgment ("Mm-hm.", "Okay."), next checklist item — efficient but with no rapport-building turns; the patient's guardedness sits above the database median for comparable abdominal-pain presentations.
sentiment.calibration_note: Score recalibrates nightly as newly captured conversations are scored — recent encounters weight the reference distribution, so comparable interactions converge over time.
